extracted_rules.condition: Always — when collecting HRV data for training readiness assessment
extracted_rules.action: Measure RMSSD each morning: supine position, 5-minute recording, consistent timing within ±30 minutes
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Protocol: supine, 5-min recording, ±30 min consistent timing; use RMSSD (ln-RMSSD or raw ms); avoid measurement after alcohol, illness, or travel on the same morning
extracted_rules.source: Plews et al. 2013 HRV4Training guidelines; HRV monitoring consensus

extracted_rules.condition: If morning RMSSD is above 20ms (or within 8% of athlete's baseline)
extracted_rules.action: Training readiness is normal; proceed with planned session; no modification required
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: RMSSD >20ms (absolute) OR within ±8% of athlete's 7-day rolling baseline → no modification
extracted_rules.source: Plews et al. 2013; HRV4Training longitudinal research

extracted_rules.condition: If morning RMSSD is 15-20ms OR drops >8% below athlete's rolling baseline on a single morning
extracted_rules.action: Flag reduced readiness; reduce planned session intensity by 15-20%; avoid new intensity PRs; reassess next morning
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: RMSSD 15-20ms OR >8% below baseline (single day): reduce intensity 15-20%; no new intensity records; reassess day+1
extracted_rules.source: Plews et al. 2013; Flatt et al. 2017 HRV-guided training

extracted_rules.condition: If morning RMSSD is <15ms OR drops >8% below baseline for 2+ consecutive mornings
extracted_rules.action: Mandatory recovery day; replace planned session with active recovery only (Z1 effort, ≤30 min); do not prescribe intensity work until RMSSD returns to baseline
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: RMSSD <15ms OR >8% below baseline for ≥2 consecutive days: mandatory rest/recovery day; Z1 only ≤30 min; no threshold or interval work
extracted_rules.source: Plews et al. 2013; Flatt et al. 2017; overtraining syndrome prevention research

extracted_rules.action: Apply veto: complete rest day; no training of any kind; assess for illness, overtraining, or acute stressor; escalate if pattern persists >3 days
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: RMSSD <10ms: complete rest (no training); check for illness/OTS indicators; if <10ms for ≥3 consecutive days: refer for medical assessment
extracted_rules.source: HRV thresholds from clinical and sports science research; overtraining syndrome criteria

extracted_rules.condition: If athlete's 7-day average HRV drops more than 8% from their longer-term (21-28 day) baseline
extracted_rules.action: Flag potential overreaching; reduce total training load by 15-20% for the current week; monitor closely
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: 7-day avg HRV drop >8% vs. 21-28 day baseline → overreaching flag; reduce weekly load 15-20%; reassess after 5-7 days of reduced load
extracted_rules.source: Plews et al. 2013; Flatt & Esco 2016 HRV trending in athletes

extracted_rules.condition: If RMSSD is <15ms AND sleep duration was <6h the same night
extracted_rules.action: Apply compound recovery flag: mandatory rest or active recovery only; both signals together indicate significantly elevated injury and illness risk
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: RMSSD <15ms + sleep <6h = compound flag: rest day mandatory; no training; prioritize sleep before reassessment
extracted_rules.source: Combined HRV and sleep research; Fullagar et al. 2015 sleep and athletic performance

extracted_rules.condition: If explaining the physiological basis of HRV monitoring to an athlete or coach
extracted_rules.action: Note that elevated vagally-mediated HRV (RMSSD) reflects parasympathetic dominance and indicates physiological recovery readiness
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: High RMSSD = parasympathetic dominance = recovery; low RMSSD = sympathetic dominance = stress/fatigue; RMSSD is the most robust short-term HRV metric
extracted_rules.source: Systematic review: HRV monitoring, stress and recovery (original recovery_coach_hrv_rules source)

extracted_rules.rule_name: Cold water immersion for HRV and PNS reactivation
extracted_rules.condition: After a high-intensity or high-volume training session where rapid recovery is prioritized
extracted_rules.action: Apply cold water immersion (CWI) as the recovery technique; produces a moderate-to-large positive effect on RMSSD recovery speed
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: CWI: 10-15°C water, 10-15 min immersion; RMSSD effect: Hedges' g = 0.75 (moderate-to-large); most effective after resistance exercise (g = 0.69)
extracted_rules.source: Meta-analysis: physical recovery techniques and vagally-mediated HRV (original recovery_coach_hrv_rules source)

extracted_rules.rule_name: Recovery techniques effectiveness after resistance exercise
extracted_rules.action: Apply active physical recovery techniques (CWI, massage, compression, active cool-down); these show larger HRV recovery effects after resistance than after continuous cardio
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Post-resistance recovery techniques: RMSSD effect g = 0.69; post-cardiovascular intermittent: g = 0.52; post-continuous cardio: no significant effect
extracted_rules.source: Meta-analysis: physical recovery techniques and vagally-mediated HRV (original recovery_coach_hrv_rules source)

extracted_rules.condition: If athlete shows chronic HRV depression (>2 weeks of below-baseline RMSSD) combined with performance decline
extracted_rules.action: Assess for overtraining syndrome (OTS); systematic HRV alteration + performance decline + fatigue = OTS criteria; prescribe extended recovery block
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: OTS indicators: chronic RMSSD depression >2 weeks + performance decline >5% + RPE inflation; if ≥2 criteria met: 2-4 week recovery block with ≤MEV training loads
extracted_rules.source: Systematic review: HRV and overtraining in soccer (original recovery_coach_hrv_rules source); Meeusen et al. 2013

extracted_rules.condition: When morning RMSSD reading is ambiguous or inconsistent with athlete's subjective feel
extracted_rules.action: Apply orthostatic HRV test: measure RMSSD supine for 2 minutes, then stand and measure for 2 more minutes; elevated HR delta signals sympathetic elevation
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Orthostatic test: supine 2 min → standing 2 min; HR delta >15 bpm (supine-to-standing rise) = sympathetic elevation flag; supplement RMSSD with orthostatic HR delta when ambiguous
extracted_rules.source: Orthostatic HRV testing research; sympathetic/parasympathetic balance literature

extracted_rules.rule_name: HRV-guided periodization — daily load decision model
extracted_rules.condition: When an athlete uses daily HRV monitoring to guide session intensity decisions
extracted_rules.action: Apply HRV-guided load rule: if daily RMSSD is within ±8% of rolling baseline, train as planned; if below threshold, reduce; this model outperforms fixed programming for VO2max gains
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: HRV-guided model: RMSSD within ±8% of 7-day rolling baseline → train as planned; >8% below → reduce intensity by 15-20%; >8% above → optional increase; Kiviniemi et al. 2010: HRV-guided group +3.7 mL/kg/min VO2max vs. fixed group +1.0 mL/kg/min
extracted_rules.source: Kiviniemi et al. 2010 HRV-guided training vs. fixed programming; Plews et al. 2013 HRV monitoring consensus

extracted_rules.rule_name: Female athlete HRV baseline correction — menstrual cycle
extracted_rules.condition: When monitoring HRV in female athletes across a training month
extracted_rules.action: Apply phase-adjusted baseline: expect higher RMSSD in follicular phase and lower in luteal phase; use 28-day rolling baseline to normalize; flag only deviations vs. phase-adjusted personal baseline, not absolute values
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: RMSSD variation: follicular phase (days 1-14): baseline higher; luteal phase (days 15-28): baseline lower by ~5-10ms; 28-day rolling baseline normalizes this variation; flag thresholds apply vs. phase-adjusted baseline, not vs. global baseline
extracted_rules.source: Menstrual cycle and HRV research; Bär et al. 2004 cardiac autonomic modulation across cycle phases

extracted_rules.rule_name: ln-RMSSD vs raw RMSSD — metric selection
extracted_rules.condition: When selecting HRV metric for monitoring or comparing athletes
extracted_rules.action: Use ln-RMSSD for population-level comparisons or research contexts; use raw RMSSD for intra-athlete longitudinal monitoring; most consumer apps report raw RMSSD — this is acceptable for individual athlete tracking
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: ln-RMSSD = natural log of RMSSD (ms); preferred for population comparisons (normalizes skewed distribution); raw RMSSD: acceptable for within-athlete tracking over time; typical athlete range: 20-100ms raw; 3.0-4.6 ln-RMSSD
extracted_rules.source: Plews et al. 2013 HRV metric selection; HRV methodology consensus literature

extracted_rules.rule_name: Dehydration effect on HRV — measurement validity
extracted_rules.condition: When morning HRV reading is unusually low on a hot day, after travel, or following inadequate fluid intake
extracted_rules.action: Flag as potential dehydration artifact; do not make hard load decisions on this reading alone; ensure consistent hydration before measurement to maintain validity
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Dehydration >2% body weight: RMSSD suppressed; effect: ~5-15ms reduction in RMSSD; confound check: hot day + travel + poor intake = dehydration flag; rehydrate and re-measure next day if pattern is unexpected
extracted_rules.source: Dehydration and autonomic function research; HRV measurement reliability literature

extracted_rules.rule_name: Nocturnal HRV — superior primary signal when available
extracted_rules.condition: If athlete's wearable device provides nocturnal (sleep-time) HRV data in addition to morning measurement
extracted_rules.action: Prefer nocturnal HRV as the primary readiness signal; it reflects full recovery arc without measurement-day confounds (posture, hydration, stress); morning RMSSD serves as secondary confirmation
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Nocturnal HRV (avg RMSSD during sleep) > morning spot RMSSD for readiness prediction; if nocturnal available: use as primary; morning RMSSD: use as secondary; both below threshold = stronger compound signal
extracted_rules.source: Nocturnal HRV prediction research; Plews et al. 2017 overnight HRV vs. morning HRV comparison
